skill.name: sql-queries-tool
skill.description: Generates, optimizes, and debugs dialect-aware SQL queries for DBX Studio database connections.
skill.summary: Build safer SQL queries for DBX Studio with schema checks, limits, and dialect-aware patterns.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but the cited lines are Markdown SQL examples, DBX Studio tool names, or ordinary SQL terms. No prompt injection, shell execution, network call, file access, cryptographic routine, or malicious intent was found; publication is acceptable with a low data-access caution because the skill helps query user databases.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines contain Markdown SQL fences, SQL examples, DBX Studio tool names, and safety instructions, not Ruby shell backtick execution. No shell command invocation or command injection path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern matched Markdown backticks and inline code formatting, while the surrounding content is documentation for SQL generation. There is no executable Ruby, shell syntax, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines use ordinary documentation and SQL words such as description, DESC, describe_table, and description-like text. No hash, cipher, password storage, or cryptographic API appears in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited terms are SQL ordering and documentation vocabulary, not cryptographic operations. The file contains no code capable of selecting or executing a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited line is part of a SQL window function example that orders transaction amounts by creation time. It does not inspect host identity, operating system details, environment variables, or local network state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is clearly inside a SQL query example and has no operating system command or reconnaissance instruction. The surrounding section documents analytics query patterns.

low_findings.title: Database Data Access Requires Normal User Controls
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the AI to preview table data and run SELECT or WITH queries through DBX Studio tools. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but deployments should rely on DBX Studio permissions and avoid exposing sensitive rows unnecessarily.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool instructions explicitly include schema reads, table previews, and SELECT execution. The safety rules reduce risk, but the skill still operates on user database content.

content.user_title: Generate Safer SQL for DBX Studio
content.value_statement: Writing dialect-specific SQL across databases is slow and error-prone. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to inspect schemas, build SELECT queries, and explain results.
actual_capabilities: Creates dialect-aware SQL patterns for PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, MySQL, and SQLite.
Directs the assistant to inspect schema metadata before writing complex queries.
Encourages SELECT and WITH queries with automatic LIMIT usage for safer exploration.
Provides examples for CTEs, aggregations, and window functions.
Guides responses to show results first and SQL only when the user requests it.
limitations: It is not a database connector and depends on DBX Studio tools being available.
It does not support INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DDL, or administrative database changes.
It cannot verify data sensitivity beyond the permissions and metadata provided by DBX Studio.
It may need user clarification when schemas contain ambiguous table or column names.

use_cases.title: Answer business data questions
use_cases.description: Create safe SELECT queries that summarize orders, revenue, users, and other operational metrics.

use_cases.title: Debug application queries
use_cases.description: Inspect table structure and rewrite slow or incorrect SQL into clearer dialect-aware queries.

use_cases.title: Support database exploration
use_cases.description: Preview tables, validate column names, and produce limited queries before deeper analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Find rows safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use DBX Studio schema tools to find the table for [entity]. Return up to 100 rows that match [condition].

prompt_templates.title: Summarize metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the relevant schema, then calculate [metric] by [dimension] for [time period]. Explain the result in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SQL intent: [goal]. Check the schema, improve the query for [database dialect], and explain important tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a multi-step analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema inspection and CTEs to analyze [business question] across [tables]. Validate joins, limit previews, and summarize final findings.

output_examples.input: Show monthly revenue for the last six months.
output_examples.output: The assistant inspects order and payment tables, runs a limited aggregate query, and returns a month-by-month revenue summary.

output_examples.input: Which customers placed repeat orders this quarter?
output_examples.output: The assistant validates customer and order columns, groups orders by customer, and reports repeat-order counts with clear assumptions.

output_examples.input: Help me debug a query that returns duplicate users.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks join keys and table relationships, identifies likely duplication causes, and suggests a safer query structure.

best_practices: Inspect schema and column names before generating final SQL.
Use SELECT or WITH queries with LIMIT during exploration.
State assumptions about joins, date ranges, and database dialect.
anti_patterns: Do not run mutating or administrative database statements.
Do not query all rows unless the user explicitly requests full results.
Do not guess table names when schema tools are available.

faq.question: Which databases does this skill support?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, MySQL, and SQLite.

faq.question: Can it modify database records?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs assistants to use SELECT and WITH queries only.

faq.question: Does it require DBX Studio?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool guidance is written for DBX Studio database tools and connections.

faq.question: Will it always show the SQL query?
faq.answer: No. It prefers direct answers and shows SQL when the user asks how the result was produced.

faq.question: How does it reduce query risk?
faq.answer: It recommends schema validation, quoted identifiers, SELECT-only execution, and LIMIT clauses for exploration.

faq.question: Can it guarantee query performance?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest clearer queries, but performance depends on database size, indexes, and execution plans.
